chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper mentions a Zenodo link for the code (Reference [6]), which provides working links to public repositories containing the implementation.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation uses both physical testbeds (AWS geo-distributed testbed and Raspberry Pi embedded device testbed) and real application data collection, qualifying it as using physical/real-world deployments rather than purely theoretical or small-scale simulations.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: A clear, explicit system model is provided in Section II. A formal definition of the adversary (adaptive adversary A corrupting t < n/3 nodes) and the problem context (Byzantine faults) are detailed in Section II.A, which serves as the threat/fault model.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides a rigorous, data-driven analysis by comparing runtime and network bandwidth consumption against state-of-the-art protocols (FIN, Abraham et al.) across different $\delta$ values in both AWS and CPS testbeds.

major_weakness: The paper is exceptionally strong across all metrics; the only minor weakness is that the complexity analysis relies heavily on assumptions about input distributions ($\lambda$ parameter).
